stem:
A 26-year-old woman presents to your emergency department after taking five paracetamol tablets following an argument with her partner 2 hours ago. She tells you 'I always do this when she leaves the room, but I never really want to die, I just want it to stop hurting'. She has had over 30 similar low-lethality presentations in the last 2 years. She has documented borderline personality disorder, longstanding chronic suicidal ideation without intent or plan, multiple superficial self-harm scars, and an unstable interpersonal history. There is no current plan or means.

On examination she is reactive and engaged with you. The paracetamol nomogram plotted at the predicted 4-hour timepoint will be well below the AU treatment line and her serum paracetamol returned at 35 mg/L (treatment line at 4 hours of 150 mg/L). She has not used opioids, benzodiazepines, or alcohol concurrently. Her usual community team has organised dialectical behaviour therapy beginning next week. Her safety plan from her last admission is current. She wants to go home with her partner.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Engaged, reactive, no thought disorder, intact reality testing, no current suicidal intent or plan, no acute intoxication
data_table.Investigations: Serum paracetamol 35 mg/L at 4 hours post ingestion (treatment line 150 mg/L); ECG QTc 410 ms; UEC and LFTs within normal limits; urine drug screen negative
data_table.Medications: Sertraline 100 mg orally daily; community DBT enrolled

lead_in: What is the most appropriate disposition?

options.text: Discharge with a brief safety plan review, partner involvement, and continuation of community DBT and case management
options.rationale: Recurrent low-lethality self-harm in established borderline personality disorder is managed in the community with dialectical behaviour therapy as the AU evidence-based intervention. Repeated admissions reinforce maladaptive coping and erode therapy alliance. Brief reaffirmation of the safety plan, partner involvement, and the next DBT session is the appropriate disposition for a presentation below the paracetamol treatment line with no current intent. (Source: RANZCP CPG BPD; NHMRC BPD Guideline)

options.text: Admit involuntarily to the acute psychiatric ward under the mental health act for 72-hour observation today
options.rationale: Involuntary admission for chronic suicidality in BPD without acute change risks reinforcing self-harm pattern and contravenes least-restrictive-care principles; reserve for acute risk escalation. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline)

options.text: Commence intravenous N-acetylcysteine immediately on a 21-hour standard regimen given the recent ingestion
options.rationale: N-acetylcysteine is indicated only when serum paracetamol is above the AU treatment line on the nomogram; this sample is below the line. Empirical NAC exposes her to flushing, anaphylactoid reactions, and admission without benefit. (Source: TG: Toxicology; Australian Resuscitation Council)

options.text: Add an antipsychotic such as quetiapine 100 mg nightly to her sertraline as the next pharmacological step today
options.rationale: Routine antipsychotic for BPD is not supported by evidence; pharmacotherapy targets specific comorbidity (depression, anxiety, severe transient psychotic symptoms) and is adjunctive to psychotherapy. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline; AMH)

options.text: Refer to inpatient detoxification given the recurrent presentations without confirmed substance use in the recent samples taken
options.rationale: Detoxification is irrelevant when urine drug screen is negative and there is no substance use disorder; this misuses inpatient resources. (Source: RANZCP CPG BPD)

explanation.summary: Borderline personality disorder is managed primarily with structured psychotherapy (dialectical behaviour therapy or mentalisation-based therapy) and assertive community case management. Repeated involuntary admissions for chronic suicidality erode therapy alliance, reinforce self-harm patterns, and breach least-restrictive-care principles. Brief admissions are reserved for clearly out-of-pattern risk escalation. Pharmacotherapy targets comorbid disorders rather than treating BPD per se; antipsychotic addition for emotion dysregulation has weak evidence. Brief targeted ED management with safety plan reaffirmation is the AU standard.
key_points: DBT is the AU evidence-based first-line psychotherapy for BPD
Involuntary admissions for chronic suicidality reinforce maladaptive patterns
Pharmacotherapy targets comorbidity, not BPD itself
Brief out-of-pattern presentations warrant admission - chronic patterns do not
explanation.pearls: Dialectical behaviour therapy reduces self-harm, suicide attempts, and admission days in BPD - the AU first-line evidence-based intervention, with 12-month structured group plus individual therapy.

stem:
A 52-year-old woman attends your community mental health clinic for routine review of bipolar I disorder. She has been on lithium 1000 mg orally at bedtime for 8 years and has been euthymic with no admissions in that time. Her partner says 'she's been more thirsty and going to the toilet more at night, but it's been gradual'. She has not had any other new symptoms, no falls, and no infections. Her medications include lithium, an ACE inhibitor for hypertension, and an oral contraceptive pill.

Routine bloods today show lithium level 0.7 mmol/L (0.4-1.0), eGFR 55 mL/min/1.73 m^2 (over 90), creatinine 110 micromol/L, and serum sodium 142 mmol/L. Her eGFR has dropped from 80 mL/min/1.73 m^2 6 years ago, with 65 mL/min/1.73 m^2 12 months ago. Urine osmolality after overnight water deprivation is 380 mOsm/kg. Thyroid function and calcium are within normal limits. Her psychiatric history shows 5 prior depressive episodes and 2 manic episodes before lithium initiation.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76, BP 138/84 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: No tremor, no nystagmus, no ataxia, no goitre, no signs of dehydration
data_table.Investigations: Lithium 0.7 mmol/L (0.4-1.0), eGFR 55 mL/min/1.73 m^2 (over 90), creatinine 110 micromol/L; urine osmolality post-water deprivation 380 mOsm/kg; TSH 2.1 mIU/L, corrected calcium 2.40 mmol/L; sodium 142 mmol/L
data_table.Medications: Lithium carbonate 1000 mg orally at bedtime, perindopril 5 mg daily, combined oral contraceptive pill

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue lithium at the same dose and repeat all bloods in 6 months without specialist involvement
options.rationale: Stage 3a chronic kidney disease with progressive decline and reduced urine concentrating ability on a stable lithium level warrants formal renal review, not routine 6-monthly follow-up alone. (Source: RANZCP CPG Bipolar; AMH)

options.text: Stop lithium immediately, swap to oral sodium valproate, and re-evaluate renal function 4 weeks after cessation
options.rationale: Abrupt lithium cessation risks early relapse (rebound mania within 6 months in 50%); valproate is teratogenic in a 52-year-old still potentially of childbearing potential and is less effective for prophylaxis than lithium. Abrupt change is also unnecessary at stable level. (Source: RANZCP CPG Bipolar; AMH)

options.text: Switch immediately to clozapine and reduce the perindopril dose to half pending renal nephrologist review
options.rationale: Clozapine is not a primary mood stabiliser and carries severe haematologic and metabolic risks; ACE inhibitor adjustment alone does not address the lithium-renal trajectory. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Refer to nephrology, discuss lithium dose reduction and timing as a single nightly dose with shared decision making
options.rationale: Progressive lithium-associated chronic kidney disease (eGFR 80 to 55 over 6 years) with impaired urine concentration suggests early nephrogenic diabetes insipidus. AU practice is shared-decision review with nephrology; lithium can often continue at lower dose given as a single nightly dose (reduced trough nephrotoxicity), with closer surveillance. Switching to a less effective alternative carries its own relapse risk and is decided jointly. (Source: RANZCP CPG Bipolar; KHA-CARI; AMH)

options.text: Commence amiloride 5 mg daily and continue lithium with the same total daily dose and same divided schedule
options.rationale: Amiloride can reduce lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus polyuria, but it does not modify the underlying nephropathy and is adjunctive; nephrology review should guide the overall plan. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Long-term lithium use is associated with nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and chronic tubulointerstitial nephropathy in around 20 to 30% of patients after 10 to 20 years. Surveillance includes 6-monthly lithium level, eGFR, electrolytes, thyroid function, and calcium. Once-nightly dosing reduces renal exposure compared to divided schedules. Stage 3 or progressive CKD on lithium warrants nephrology review and shared-decision balancing relapse risk against renal trajectory. Lithium remains the most effective mood stabiliser for bipolar prophylaxis - switching is not automatic.
key_points: Once-nightly lithium dosing reduces renal exposure compared to divided
Stage 3+ CKD on lithium: refer nephrology, shared decision making
Surveillance: lithium, eGFR, electrolytes, TSH, calcium every 6 months
Switching carries relapse risk - rarely automatic
explanation.pearls: Lithium given as a single nightly dose reduces the renal tubular trough exposure compared with twice-daily dosing; consider switching schedules before reducing dose in early lithium nephrotoxicity.

stem:
A 38-year-old man is brought to your emergency department by his housemate with 12 hours of confusion, sweating, tremor, and a temperature of 39.5 °C. He has a history of treatment-resistant depression and obsessive-compulsive disorder. His housemate says 'he started a new tablet last week and was telling me he was anxious and not sleeping right'. His regular medications are escitalopram 20 mg, mirtazapine 30 mg, and quetiapine 100 mg at night. Five days ago his psychiatrist commenced linezolid for a methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus soft tissue infection. There has been no recent dose increase of antipsychotic.

On examination he is restless, with diaphoresis, mydriasis, and bilateral lower limb myoclonus that is more pronounced than the upper limbs. Tendon reflexes are 3+ symmetrically and clonus is sustained at both ankles. Tone is increased in the lower limbs but lead-pipe rigidity is absent. Bowel sounds are hyperactive. ECG shows sinus tachycardia at 124 bpm with normal QRS and QTc. Creatine kinase is 410 U/L (under 200), white cell count 11 x10^9/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 124, BP 158/96 mmHg, RR 24, SpO2 96% on room air, temperature 39.5 °C
data_table.Examination: Restless, diaphoretic, mydriasis, lower limb myoclonus greater than upper, reflexes 3+, ankle clonus sustained, lower limb tone increased without lead-pipe rigidity, hyperactive bowel sounds
data_table.Investigations: CK 410 U/L (under 200), WCC 11 x10^9/L (4.0-11.0), Na 138 mmol/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L; ECG sinus tachycardia, normal QRS and QTc
data_table.Medications: Escitalopram 20 mg, mirtazapine 30 mg, quetiapine 100 mg at night, linezolid started 5 days ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Bromocriptine 2.5 mg orally three times daily as dopamine agonist therapy for neuroleptic malignant syndrome
options.rationale: NMS features lead-pipe rigidity, hyporeflexia or normal reflexes, slow onset over days to weeks, much higher CK, and pallid skin rather than diaphoresis and hyperactive bowel sounds; this presentation is not NMS. Bromocriptine is the dopamine-agonist NMS treatment but is the wrong tool here. (Source: TG: Toxicology; AMH)

options.text: Dantrolene 2 mg/kg intravenously now repeated as needed for the rigidity and the hyperthermia today
options.rationale: Dantrolene is reserved for severe NMS and malignant hyperthermia; the syndrome here matches serotonin syndrome (clonus, hyperreflexia, lower-limb predominance, mydriasis, hyperactive bowel sounds), and antiserotoninergic management is preferred. (Source: TG: Toxicology)

options.text: Cease serotonergic agents including linezolid, aggressive cooling and benzodiazepine sedation, and consider cyproheptadine
options.rationale: Sustained ankle clonus, hyperreflexia with lower limb predominance, mydriasis, diaphoresis, and hyperactive bowel sounds within 24 hours of adding a serotonergic agent (linezolid is a monoamine oxidase inhibitor; combined with escitalopram and mirtazapine creates triple serotonergic exposure) fulfils Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria. AU management is removal of offending agents, aggressive cooling, intravenous benzodiazepine sedation for agitation and tone, supportive care, and cyproheptadine 12 mg orally then 2 mg every 2 hours for moderate to severe cases unresponsive to supportive measures. (Source: TG: Toxicology; Royal Australasian College of Physicians)

options.text: Empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics for sepsis and admit to the intensive care unit for invasive monitoring this evening
options.rationale: Sepsis can mimic this picture but the clonus and hyperreflexia pattern with serotonergic exposure history points to serotonin syndrome; antibiotic cover does not address the toxic mechanism even if added empirically. (Source: TG: Toxicology)

options.text: Administer flumazenil 0.2 mg intravenously now for the agitation and arrange psychiatric ward transfer today
options.rationale: Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines and can precipitate seizure; it has no role and is not relevant here. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Serotonin syndrome and neuroleptic malignant syndrome can both present with hyperthermia, altered mental state, and autonomic instability, but the discriminators are clinically actionable. Serotonin syndrome (Hunter criteria) presents with clonus, hyperreflexia, lower-limb predominance, mydriasis, diaphoresis, and hyperactive bowel sounds within 24 hours of a serotonergic increase or interaction (such as linezolid, an irreversible monoamine oxidase inhibitor, added to a serotonergic regimen). NMS presents with lead-pipe rigidity, hyporeflexia, dry skin, marked CK elevation, and slower onset over days. Management diverges: serotonin syndrome - cease agents, cool, benzodiazepines, cyproheptadine. NMS - cease antipsychotic, cool, supportive care, bromocriptine or dantrolene in severe cases.
key_points: Hyperreflexia + clonus + mydriasis + GI hyperactivity = serotonin syndrome
Lead-pipe rigidity + hyporeflexia + huge CK + dry skin = NMS
Linezolid is a monoamine oxidase inhibitor: never combine with SSRI
Cyproheptadine for moderate-severe serotonin syndrome not responding to benzodiazepines
explanation.pearls: Cyproheptadine 12 mg orally as a loading dose then 2 mg every 2 hours up to 32 mg per day is the AU antiserotoninergic step for moderate-severe serotonin syndrome unresponsive to benzodiazepine sedation and cooling.

stem:
A 64-year-old man presents to your emergency department with 3 days of productive cough, fever, and pleuritic right-sided chest pain. He says 'I get out of breath just walking from the kitchen to the toilet now'. He has well-controlled hypertension and is an ex-smoker (40 pack-years, quit 6 years ago). He has had no recent travel, no aspiration risk, no immunosuppression, and no prior pneumonia.

On examination he is alert. The chest has right basal crackles with bronchial breathing and dullness to percussion. There is no asterixis. Chest X-ray shows right lower lobe consolidation without effusion. Investigations: WBC 16 x10^9/L (4.0-11.0), CRP 220 mg/L (<5), albumin 36 g/L, urea 8.5 mmol/L, sodium 135 mmol/L, creatinine 102 micromol/L, arterial pH 7.42, PaO2 65 mmHg on room air, PaCO2 38 mmHg. SMART-COP score is 3 (low albumin, multilobar nil; respiratory rate, tachycardia, confusion nil; oxygen, pH).

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 108, BP 124/76 mmHg, RR 26, SpO2 92% on room air, temperature 38.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Right basal crackles with bronchial breathing, dullness to percussion, no peripheral oedema, no confusion
data_table.Investigations: WBC 16 x10^9/L (4.0-11.0), CRP 220 mg/L (<5), albumin 36 g/L, urea 8.5 mmol/L, sodium 135 mmol/L, creatinine 102 micromol/L; ABG pH 7.42, PaO2 65 mmHg, PaCO2 38 mmHg on room air; chest X-ray right lower lobe consolidation, no effusion

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g three times daily and oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily as an outpatient course of treatment
options.rationale: Oral monotherapy or oral combination is appropriate for low-severity CAP (SMART-COP 0-2) without admission; SMART-COP 3 in this patient (low SpO2, low pH-neutral with low PaO2 less than 70 mmHg, plus low albumin) is moderate severity warranting admission and intravenous therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 1.2 g 6-hourly plus oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily as the ward regimen
options.rationale: Moderate-severity CAP (SMART-COP 3-4, admitted to ward) is treated with AU first-line intravenous benzylpenicillin plus oral doxycycline (or oral azithromycin) to cover Streptococcus pneumoniae and atypicals (Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila). Step-down to oral amoxicillin when stable. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g 6-hourly plus intravenous azithromycin and intensive care admission
options.rationale: Anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam plus macrolide combination is reserved for severe CAP (SMART-COP 5+), severe sepsis, or specific risk factors for resistant organisms (structural lung disease, recent broad-spectrum antibiotics); SMART-COP 3 with ward admission does not need this. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 1 g daily plus intravenous vancomycin 25 mg/kg loading dose and ward admission today
options.rationale: Vancomycin empirical cover is reserved for MRSA-suspected pneumonia (severe necrotising, post-influenza secondary bacterial, intravenous drug use, or community MRSA prevalence high); none apply here. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous moxifloxacin 400 mg daily monotherapy and discharge home on completion with general practitioner review
options.rationale: Fluoroquinolone monotherapy in CAP is reserved for penicillin allergy with anaphylaxis or other contraindication; AU stewardship discourages fluoroquinolone first-line because of resistance and tendinopathy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; AMH)

explanation.summary: SMART-COP stratifies community-acquired pneumonia by need for intensive respiratory or vasopressor support. Score 0-2: low severity, outpatient amoxicillin plus doxycycline. Score 3-4: moderate severity, ward admission with IV benzylpenicillin plus oral doxycycline. Score 5+: severe, ICU consideration with IV ceftriaxone plus IV azithromycin (and anti-pseudomonal cover if structural lung disease). Step-down to oral amoxicillin or doxycycline when clinically stable; total duration 5-7 days.
key_points: SMART-COP 0-2 outpatient, 3-4 ward, 5+ ICU consideration
Ward AU first-line: IV benzylpenicillin + oral doxycycline (or azithromycin)
ICU CAP: IV ceftriaxone + IV azithromycin; add anti-pseudomonal if at risk
Total duration 5-7 days; step down when clinically stable
explanation.pearls: SMART-COP gives 2 points for systolic BP under 90 mmHg, multilobar CXR, low albumin under 35 g/L, RR raised, tachycardia over 125 bpm; and 2 points for confusion, oxygen low, low pH - a score of 3 or 4 is moderate severity requiring admission.

subtopic_detail: Iron deficiency anaemia in older adult
stem:
A 67-year-old man attends your general practice clinic with 4 months of progressive fatigue and dyspnoea on exertion. He says 'I get puffed bringing in the shopping now, when last winter I was still walking the dog up the hill'. He has not noticed any rectal bleeding, melaena, change in bowel habit, abdominal pain, or weight loss. He has no past gastrointestinal disease. He is a non-smoker, drinks 4 standard drinks weekly, and his diet is balanced.

On examination he is pale with no signs of heart failure. Per-rectal examination reveals no mass and faecal occult blood is positive on point-of-care testing. FBC shows haemoglobin 88 g/L (130-180), MCV 72 fL (80-100), MCH 24 pg (27-32). Ferritin 8 micrograms/L (30-300), transferrin saturation 7% (16-45%). LFTs and UEC are within normal limits. He is haemodynamically stable. His grandparents were Mediterranean but he has never been investigated for haemoglobinopathy; his haemoglobin 3 years ago was 142 g/L.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 84, BP 132/78 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae, no signs of heart failure, abdomen soft non-tender, no organomegaly, per-rectal no mass, faecal occult blood positive
data_table.Investigations: Haemoglobin 88 g/L (130-180), MCV 72 fL (80-100), MCH 24 pg (27-32), ferritin 8 micrograms/L (30-300), transferrin saturation 7% (16-45%); LFTs and UEC within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Empirical oral iron supplementation for 12 weeks and arrange repeat FBC and ferritin assessment then
options.rationale: Empirical iron treatment without investigating the source of loss in an adult over 50 (and any postmenopausal woman) is unsafe; colorectal malignancy must be excluded. (Source: GESA Iron Deficiency Guidelines; NHMRC NBCSP)

options.text: Test for haemoglobin electrophoresis and HbA2 for beta-thalassaemia trait given his Mediterranean family history
options.rationale: Inherited haemoglobinopathy is unlikely in an adult whose haemoglobin was 142 g/L three years ago; ferritin of 8 micrograms/L is diagnostic of iron deficiency. (Source: GESA Iron Deficiency Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for upper endoscopy and colonoscopy under one anaesthetic session for source of blood loss
options.rationale: Iron-deficiency anaemia in an adult over 50 (or any postmenopausal woman) is colorectal cancer until proven otherwise. AU practice is dual upper endoscopy and colonoscopy on the same anaesthetic to identify upper gastrointestinal pathology (peptic ulcer, malignancy) and lower gastrointestinal cancer or angiodysplasia. Empirical iron without endoscopy delays diagnosis. (Source: GESA Iron Deficiency Guidelines; NHMRC NBCSP)

options.text: Order CT colonography with intravenous contrast as the only screening investigation needed at this stage
options.rationale: CT colonography misses small adenomas and cannot biopsy or treat; colonoscopy is preferred when the index of suspicion for malignancy is high. Upper endoscopy is also needed. (Source: GESA Iron Deficiency Guidelines)

options.text: Send urea breath test for Helicobacter pylori and treat empirically with eradication if positive on testing
options.rationale: H. pylori may contribute to iron deficiency via gastritis but does not exclude malignancy; endoscopy with biopsy is required. (Source: GESA Iron Deficiency Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Iron-deficiency anaemia in adults over 50 (any sex), or in postmenopausal women, mandates urgent gastrointestinal investigation for occult malignancy. Combined upper endoscopy and colonoscopy under one anaesthetic is the AU standard. Premenopausal women with menstrual losses can be treated empirically with iron, but those with persistent or severe deficiency, family history of colorectal cancer, or other red flags warrant endoscopic workup. Once a cause is found, treat with oral iron (or IV iron if oral intolerant) and recheck at 6-12 weeks.
key_points: Adults over 50 with iron deficiency: dual endoscopy until proven otherwise
Premenopausal: treat empirically with iron unless red flags
Oral iron first-line; IV iron if oral failed or intolerant
Recheck Hb and ferritin at 6-12 weeks of treatment
explanation.pearls: Oral iron is best absorbed when taken second-daily on an empty stomach (the hepcidin rebound from daily dosing reduces absorption), often with vitamin C; treat for 3 to 6 months past haemoglobin normalisation to refill stores.
